title: Academic papers and conference materials offer a deep dive into China's all-CPU LineShine, which pairs custom 304-core Arm CPUs with HBM to top the Top500 (Timothy Prickett Morgan/The Next Platform)
blurb: China's all-CPU LineShine supercomputer pairs custom 304-core Arm CPUs with high-bandwidth memory to claim the top spot on the Top500 ranking, without using any GPUs. The design leans on HBM to feed its dense CPU cores, signaling that a CPU-only architecture can still compete at the highest tier of high-performance computing.

title: Data Exposure Flaws Threaten Dify AI Platform Used by 1 Million Apps
blurb: Researchers at Zafran Security disclosed DifyTap, a set of four vulnerabilities in the open source LLMOps platform Dify that let attackers read other tenants' private chats, files, and documents in multi-tenant deployments. Dify, which powers over 1 million applications, patched all four flaws in version 1.14.2, and the two most severe carry CVSS scores of 9.1 and 9.4.

title: US Offers $10 Million Bounty for Russian State Hackers as Messaging App Attacks Evolve
blurb: The US State Department is offering up to $10 million for information on two Russian intelligence-linked hacking groups, UNC5792 and UNC4221, that hijack Signal and WhatsApp accounts belonging to officials, military leaders, and journalists. CISA and the FBI warn the attackers now phish for Backup Recovery Keys, which stay valid even after a victim re-registers the same phone number, letting the hackers reclaim rebuilt accounts later.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic Limit New AI Models to Trump-Approved Customers During Cybersecurity Review
blurb: OpenAI is restricting its new GPT-5.6 Sol model to customers approved by the Trump administration during a federal cybersecurity review, while Anthropic won clearance to redeploy its Mythos 5 model to a small group of cyber defenders after the Commerce Department had banned it. Both moves follow Trump's June executive order letting the government vet the most advanced AI systems for up to 30 days before public release.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions that hid their payloads inside image and font files using steganography, tracing the campaign, dubbed StegoAd, to a single threat actor active since at least 2021. The extensions posed as ad blockers, VPNs, and translators across an install base of up to 2.6 million, staying dormant through evasion checks before stealing credentials and running ad fraud.

title: China’s hollow-core fiber trial pushes 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration — milestone targets AI-era networking bottlenecks
blurb: Chinese firm YOFC, with China Telecom and Dekoli, completed the first field trial of hollow-core fiber moving 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles (206.5 km) without signal regeneration, hitting 1.2 Tb/s per wavelength using only standard erbium-doped amplifiers. Hollow-core fiber guides light through air rather than glass, cutting latency and distortion, and the result advances a high-capacity networking technology largely developed outside the Western supply chain.

title: ETH Zurich’s bidirectional pixel could turn screens into cameras
blurb: Researchers at ETH Zurich built the first bidirectional pixel, published in Nature, that both emits an image and analyzes incoming light, capturing brightness, phase, and polarization from a single patch of chip. The work points toward camera-displays that show a picture and watch the viewer from one surface, though current pixels require laser light, are fixed in what they display, and remain far from scaling into a full matrix.

title: Data breach exposes up to 14.2 million email logins at six ISPs
blurb: KDDI disclosed a breach exposing email addresses and passwords for up to 14.2 million customers across six Japanese ISPs; affected users should reset passwords.

title: Crunchbase Data: Q2 Brought The Most Billion-Dollar Startup Exits Since 2021
blurb: Q2 2026 produced the most billion-dollar startup exits since the 2021 market peak, led by SpaceX's record IPO that raised $75B and hit a $2.1T first-day market cap. The bigger trend is size over count, with SpaceX's $60B purchase of Cursor marking the priciest venture-backed acquisition ever and confidential IPO filings from Anthropic and OpenAI now testing the trillion-dollar mark.

title: GPT-5.6 gets better at cybersecurity
blurb: OpenAI began rolling out its GPT-5.6 model series in limited preview to trusted partners through the API and Codex, led by the flagship Sol alongside the balanced Terra and the faster, cheaper Luna. Sol tops the Terminal-Bench 2.1 coding benchmark and advances long-horizon security tasks like vulnerability research, though OpenAI says it shipped with the company's most robust safety stack yet and coordinated the release with the U.S. government.

title: Ex-Anthropic researchers raise $200M for self-improving AI
blurb: Mirendil, founded by two researchers who left Anthropic after roughly a year, raised $200M at a $1B valuation in one of the sector's largest seed rounds, co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins with Nvidia joining. The startup wants to sell the self-improving AI that frontier labs build for their own research and bar customers from replicating, packaging an automated AI researcher that designs experiments, tunes settings, and runs training rounds.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Spend $520 Billion on Chip Plants in South Korea
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix will spend a combined $520B on new chip plants in South Korea, saying current investment levels can't meet demand amid a global shortage. The world's two largest memory-chip makers are racing to expand capacity as AI workloads strain supply, with the buildout reinforcing South Korea's central role in the memory market.

title: Trump Administration Asks OpenAI to Stagger AI Model Release
blurb: The Trump administration asked OpenAI to stagger the release of an upcoming powerful AI model, according to a person familiar with the matter. The request comes nearly two weeks after rival Anthropic pulled its most capable offerings from the market under regulatory pressure, signaling that Washington is now shaping the pace at which frontier models reach customers.

title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
blurb: GitHub detailed how Copilot stretches each token through prompt caching and on-demand tool search, plus expanding Auto to pick the model per task.

title: Kids act would require age checks to get online
blurb: The KIDS Act, which Congress may vote on within a week, bundles a revised KOSA with other internet bills under an expedited process that would pressure online services to verify every user's age. The EFF argues its "knows or should have known" liability standard for identifying minors will push platforms toward blanket age-checking, undermining privacy, encryption, and free expression for users of all ages.

title: Intel's next-gen 52-core Nova Lake CPU could pull up to 474W — high-end LGA1954 motherboards may need three 8-pin power connectors to feed the monster
blurb: Leaked specs put Intel's flagship 52-core Nova Lake desktop CPU at a 474W PL2 boost limit, with a dual-compute-tile design pairing 16 performance cores and 32 efficiency cores on the new LGA1954 socket. Enthusiast Z990 boards may add a third EPS 8-pin connector for extreme overclocking, while Intel's bLLC cache and DDR5-8000 support target AMD's 3D V-Cache gaming lead.

title: Google's new hand-wave reCAPTCHA can be bypassed with a stock photo
blurb: Google is testing a new reCAPTCHA that asks users to wave at their webcam, recording a short video and extracting 21 hand-knuckle coordinates to confirm they're human. Early testers bypassed it in minutes by feeding a static stock photo of a waving hand through OBS Virtual Camera, exposing both a trivial security gap and privacy concerns over capturing what qualifies as biometric data.

title: Anthropic Wants Claude to Be Your New Slack Coworker
blurb: Anthropic released Claude Tag, a Slack feature that lets Claude act on a user's behalf across an entire channel. The tool monitors channel activity, sends preset alerts about posts that may affect the user's day, drops comments into conversations, and can be directed to fix code issues, pushing Claude further into persistent workplace-agent territory.

title: Sources: CXMT and Tencent sign a ~$3B, three-year DRAM supply agreement for servers ahead of CXMT's IPO; CXMT is in talks with other major Chinese companies (Reuters)
blurb: CXMT signed a roughly $3B, three-year deal to supply server DRAM to Tencent ahead of its planned IPO, according to Reuters sources. The Chinese memory maker is in talks with other major domestic companies, a sign Beijing's push for homegrown chips is locking in large local buyers as it scales DRAM production.

title: We’re making Bunny DNS free
blurb: Bunny.net eliminated all DNS query fees, offering free hosting for up to 500 domains with no query limits or per-request billing.

title: OpenAI may delay its IPO to 2027 to hold out for $1tn
blurb: OpenAI is weighing an IPO as late as 2027 as Altman holds out for a $1 trillion valuation, having reportedly rejected an earlier debut at a lower price, Bloomberg reported. The delay sent SoftBank shares down as much as 13% and dinged Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs on deferred fees, while ceding ground to Anthropic, which has also filed to go public.

title: Prosecutors used ChatGPT logs as evidence in the Palisades fire trial
blurb: Prosecutors in the Palisades fire arson trial entered Jonathan Rinderknecht's ChatGPT logs as evidence, citing prompts that generated fire images and asked whether a person could be blamed for a fire started by their cigarette. Jurors were unconvinced, deadlocking 10-2 for the defense and forcing a mistrial, with one juror saying she talks to ChatGPT constantly and saw the logs as proof of nothing.

title: AWS raises GPU prices 20% as the memory crunch bites
blurb: AWS raised prices for EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML by roughly 20% starting in July, the second increase in six months after a 15% bump in January. The hike hits only the reserved Nvidia GPU blocks used by teams training large models, spares Amazon's in-house Trainium chips, and reflects a shortage of high-bandwidth memory that gives cloud giants pricing power to pass higher costs to customers.

title: OpenAI hires Apple’s Vision Pro chief Paul Meade
blurb: OpenAI hired Paul Meade, the Apple vice president who led Vision Pro hardware engineering for seven years, to work on its planned family of AI devices. Meade is the most senior Apple defection yet and rejoins former colleagues Jony Ive, Tang Tan, and Evans Hankey, whose hardware startup OpenAI bought for $6.5B last year, reassembling Apple's old hardware brain trust inside an AI lab.

title: China’s Loongson launches homegrown 16-core server CPU built on LoongArch architecture — 40W chip with DDR4 ECC and 32 PCIe lanes targets cheap SMB file, database, and web servers
blurb: Loongson released the 3C3000, a 16-core server CPU built on its homegrown LoongArch instruction set and aimed at low-cost file, database, and web servers for small and medium businesses. The 40W chip runs at 1.5GHz to 1.8GHz with dual DDR4-2400 ECC channels and 32 PCIe lanes, and is pin-compatible with the earlier 3B6000 so builders can reuse existing platforms.

title: DeepSeek’s $7.4bn raise is one symptom of China’s AI cash surge
blurb: DeepSeek closed a $7.4bn round at a valuation above $50bn, the largest first-time raise by a Chinese startup, after three years running solely on founder Liang Wenfeng's own wealth. The round reflects a broader surge that has China minting a new unicorn every five days, with US export curbs and model bans appearing to accelerate rather than slow domestic AI spending.

title: China Births Two Robot Unicorns as Sector Funding Stays Strong
blurb: Two Chinese robotics startups reached valuations above $2.9B in recent funding rounds, adding two new unicorns as investor interest in the sector holds steady. The rounds strengthen China's push to compete with US humanoid-robot firms like Tesla and Figure AI.

title: Pollen (CEO Negus-Fancey, CTO Wright) tried to remove article, and Google helped
blurb: Pragmatic Engineer author Gergely Orosz reports that Google removed his 2022 investigation of failed events startup Pollen from search results after a copyright claim he says is bogus, since he is the sole copyright holder. The case shows how anyone can file a fraudulent DMCA notice to scrub unflattering coverage from Google's index, leaving the actual author to fight a slow appeals process.

title: Cloudflare Collaborates With Leading Browsers to Develop a Privacy-First Protocol For the Global Internet
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are developing PACT, a privacy-preserving protocol to verify human and bot traffic without invasive tracking.

title: Musk approved to acquire optical transceiver developer taking on Chinese dominance
blurb: The FTC cleared Elon Musk to acquire Mesh Optical Technologies, a startup founded by ex-SpaceX engineers building optical transceivers to challenge China's dominance of the market. Mesh emerged from stealth in February after a Thrive Capital-led Series A, and its Alpha C1 transceiver moves data at 1.6 Tb/s while drawing roughly a third of rivals' power, targeting the high-speed interconnects AI hyperscalers need.

title: Austria Lobbies EU to Host Anthropic After US Access Curbs
blurb: Austria is lobbying the EU to host Anthropic within its borders, a response to US efforts to block foreign access to the most advanced American AI models. The push reflects growing European anxiety over AI sovereignty, positioning the bloc to secure direct access to frontier systems rather than depend on US export policy.

title: Google Cloud to Offer Specialist AI Models for Science Research
blurb: Google Cloud will offer SandboxAQ's specialist AI models to enterprise and research customers, targeting drug discovery, materials science, and semiconductor manufacturing. The deal widens access to domain-specific models built for scientific work, extending Google's cloud strategy beyond its general-purpose Gemini lineup into vertical research applications.

title: Comcast Plans to Spin Off NBCUniversal Into New Media Firm
blurb: Comcast plans to spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a new publicly traded media company, separating its content businesses from cable-TV and internet operations. The move aims to lift shareholder value as legacy pay-TV declines, leaving Comcast focused on broadband and the new firm to compete in streaming against Netflix and Disney.

title: SpaceX's $25 billion bond sale drives huge demand - and a potential headache for investors
blurb: SpaceX raised $25B in senior unsecured notes, drawing nearly $90B in orders just two weeks after its record IPO. The offering repays a bridge loan but underscores the company's $5B net loss and doubled capex, while analysts warn that holding both SpaceX equity and bonds concentrates the same execution risk rather than diversifying it.

title: OpenAI Restricts GPT-5.6 Release to Government-Approved Partners
blurb: OpenAI restricted access to its new GPT-5.6 family to roughly 20 partners approved by the Trump administration, the first time the US government has preemptively limited a domestic model before release. The company shipped Sol, Terra, and Luna through its API and Codex but not ChatGPT, and objected publicly to the customer-by-customer approval while complying as a short-term step under Trump's cybersecurity executive order.

title: IP Crawl: Living atlas of open webcams discovered on the public internet
blurb: A searchable atlas of open webcams found exposed on the public internet, indexed live by location and ISP.

title: Dissecting Apple's Sparse Image Format (ASIF)
blurb: A reverse engineer documents how they wrote a parser for ASIF, the sparse virtual disk format Apple introduced in macOS 26 Tahoe.

title: Chinese robotics startup AI² Robotics raised ~$736M at a ~$2.9B valuation; Alibaba-backed X Square Robot raised an undisclosed amount at a ~$2.9B valuation (Bloomberg)
blurb: Two Chinese robotics startups raised funding at roughly $2.9B valuations, with AI² Robotics pulling in about $736M and Alibaba-backed X Square Robot raising an undisclosed sum, per Bloomberg. The near-simultaneous rounds underscore how aggressively Chinese investors are funding humanoid and embodied-AI ventures as the sector races to match US robotics players.

title: An interview with Axon CEO Rick Smith, who transformed the Taser maker into a policing software company, as its revenue from AI policing tools rises 700%+ YoY (Victoria Albert/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Axon CEO Rick Smith has reshaped the former Taser maker into a policing software company, with revenue from its AI tools rising more than 700% year over year. In a new interview, Smith details the shift toward software and recurring revenue, a transition that now positions Axon's AI products as the company's fastest-growing business and a core part of its future.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix at Center of South Korea's AI Drive
blurb: South Korea is coordinating at least $880B in investment from Samsung Electronics, SK Hynix, and other domestic firms to build AI infrastructure it calls essential to competing in the AI era. The push leans on the country's memory-chip leaders, with Samsung and SK Hynix supplying the high-bandwidth memory that powers AI accelerators and anchoring a national bet on data centers and advanced manufacturing.

title: SpaceX wants its own gas pipeline to fuel Starship
blurb: SpaceX filed plans in Texas to build Starpipe, an eight-mile natural gas pipeline feeding liquid methane to Starship at its Starbase launch site, with construction possibly starting next month. The line would replace the hundreds of fuel tankers needed per launch, supporting Musk's goal of scaling from 12 Starship tests to thousands of launches a year as part of a deeper push into propellant drilling and processing.

title: China Pushes All School Levels to Teach AI in Xi’s Tech Drive
blurb: China unveiled a national plan to embed AI instruction across every level of schooling, from primary grades through university, as part of Xi Jinping's push to lead in advanced technology. The policy ties curriculum reform directly to state industrial strategy, aiming to build a domestic talent pipeline as competition with the US over AI capability intensifies.

title: Apple may struggle to get clearance for Chinese RAM, even for Chinese iPhones
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for permission to buy memory chips from blacklisted Chinese manufacturers CXMT and YMTC, even for iPhones sold only in China. The push faces long odds: a similar 2022 request under Biden drew bipartisan Senate opposition, and acting National Security Advisor Marco Rubio was among the lawmakers who signed a letter warning that YMTC's CCP ties posed supply-chain security risks.

title: AI Models Capable of Devastating Attacks on Governments and Businesses are Months Away
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance issued a rare joint statement warning that AI models capable of devastating cyberattacks on governments and businesses are months, not years, away, and urged leaders to act now. The agencies said frontier models will sharply increase the speed, scale, and sophistication of attacks by lowering barriers for bad actors, framing cyber risk as a core business and leadership responsibility rather than a purely technical one.

title: Config 2026: New Materials, New Tools, and a More Expressive Canvas
blurb: Figma recapped its Config 2026 announcements, centered on code layers that turn any design layer into an interactive, prompt-editable code block on the canvas. The release also adds Figma Motion, shaders, generative plugins, and Weave tools, all composable in the same multiplayer file, with the ability to extract design frames back into editable layers when needed.

title: Sources: disconnected US military databases may have led to the February 28 strike on an Iranian school; some see AI as a fix, others fear it amplifies errors (Katrina Manson/Los Angeles Times)
blurb: Disconnected US military databases may have contributed to the February 28 strike on an Iranian school, according to sources cited by the Los Angeles Times. The episode has split observers, with some arguing AI could reconcile fragmented intelligence systems and others warning it would amplify the same errors at machine speed.

title: Verizon and the UK's BT agree to create a joint venture for their international businesses with ~$4B in combined yearly revenue; Verizon will pay $625M to BT (Bloomberg)
blurb: Verizon and the UK's BT agreed to merge their international enterprise businesses into a joint venture with roughly $4B in combined annual revenue, with Verizon paying BT $625M for the deal. The venture combines the two carriers' multinational networking and connectivity operations, consolidating a segment where both have struggled to compete against larger global players.

title: Tumbling AI stocks signal another day of turmoil for tech companies
blurb: Tech stocks sold off Friday on mounting concern over rising AI infrastructure costs, with SoftBank plunging more than 12% and Apple shedding 6% after raising MacBook and iPad prices. The weakness spread across the global chip supply chain, hitting SK Hynix, Samsung, and TSMC, as investors weighed whether climbing semiconductor prices will squeeze margins at the largest tech companies.

title: Trump threatens 100% tariffs over digital services taxes
blurb: Trump threatened a 100% tariff on all goods from any country that imposes a digital services tax on US tech firms, posting that the levy would override existing trade deals including the EU's newly approved Turnberry agreement. The European Commission called taxation a "sovereign right" and vowed to respond, while the threat's legal footing is shaky after the Supreme Court struck down Trump's reciprocal tariffs this year.

title: Tesla settles a fatal Full Self-Driving crash lawsuit
blurb: Tesla settled a lawsuit tied to a fatal 2023 crash in which a Model Y running Full Self-Driving (Supervised) struck and killed a 71-year-old pedestrian on an Arizona highway, the first known pedestrian death linked to its automation. Terms were not disclosed, and the settlement leaves intact a federal NHTSA probe that escalated to an engineering analysis in March 2026, the final step before a possible recall.

title: China establishes VLEO industry alliance as satellites demonstrate sustained low-orbit operations
blurb: China established a national VLEO Technology Innovation and Industry Development Alliance, co-founded by 34 universities, research institutes, and commercial space firms, as two of its satellites sustain operations below 300 kilometers. The demonstrator Shiyan-25 has held an altitude near 270 kilometers since September 2023, where atmospheric drag is roughly 10 times greater than at the ISS, signaling progress on the propulsion needed for high-resolution, low-latency orbits.

title: Google reportedly capped Meta's use of Gemini AI for coding and chatbots
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini models after Meta exceeded its allotted compute capacity, sources told the Financial Times, prompting Google to warn the company in March and Meta to ask staff to use tokens more efficiently. Meta, which runs no cloud business of its own, had picked Gemini over its own Llama models for coding, customer service, and advertiser chatbots, and has pledged $600B in cloud investments over two years.

title: India's Tata Electronics Hit By Cybersecurity Breach Claiming to Expose Apple, Tesla Trade Secrets
blurb: Tata Electronics, a key Apple and Tesla supplier, was hit by a cyberattack in which an attacker claims to have stolen internal documents and trade secrets from both companies. The breach raises supply-chain security concerns for the iPhone assembler as it expands manufacturing in India, though Tata has not confirmed the scope or whether customer data was compromised.

title: New Alexa+ Agentic Ads let shoppers purchase directly within an ad
blurb: Amazon introduced Alexa+ Agentic Ads, the first ad format that lets shoppers complete a purchase through conversation without leaving the ad. The format launches during Prime Day with Papa Johns for food ordering and artists Beck, Jill Scott, and Omar Courtz for Ticketmaster concert sales, with Amazon planning to add more brands over time.

title: Micron and Anthropic Announce Strategic Agreement to Scale Next-Generation AI Infrastructure
blurb: Micron announced a strategic agreement with Anthropic that covers joint memory and storage architecture design, a multi-year supply deal for Micron's data center HBM, DRAM, and SSDs, and an investment in Anthropic's Series H round. The partnership ties frontier model demand directly to infrastructure, giving Anthropic guaranteed supply for training and serving Claude while positioning Micron's memory portfolio at the center of AI compute scaling.

title: iPhone Ultra 3D-printed hinge problems reportedly solved, ready for September launch
blurb: Apple has resolved the durability and tolerance problems with the 3D-printed hinge on its first foldable, the iPhone Ultra, clearing the device for a September launch, according to a new supply-chain report in The Elec. Hinge modules from Taiwan's Shinjuxing and US-based Amphenol caused noise and defect-rate issues during testing, but the phone has now entered test production ahead of mass manufacturing.

title: Grammarly parent Superhuman buys AI detector GPTZero
blurb: Superhuman, the company formerly known as Grammarly, agreed to acquire AI-detection startup GPTZero for an undisclosed sum. GPTZero brings 19 million registered users, roughly $30M in annual recurring revenue, and tools that flag machine-written text, fake citations, and invented statistics, all of which Superhuman will fold into its Go assistant as part of an "authenticity layer" for verifying where content came from.

title: Microsoft’s $570 Billion Rout Sets Up Its Worst Month Since 2000
blurb: Microsoft shares are on track for their worst month since 2000, shedding about $570B in market value as investors question how the company will hold up in an AI-driven software market. The selloff puts Microsoft on pace for its steepest monthly decline since the dot-com crash, reflecting growing doubt that its AI investments will protect its core franchises.

title: Rocket Lab Buys Satellite Operator Iridium in Bid to Challenge SpaceX
blurb: Rocket Lab agreed to acquire satellite operator Iridium, giving it control of a global communications fleet and the spectrum rights to build a network rivaling SpaceX's Starlink. The deal pushes Rocket Lab beyond launch into satellite services, positioning the company to compete across the full space stack rather than just ferrying others' payloads to orbit.

title: Comcast is splitting in two
blurb: Comcast announced it will split into two publicly traded companies, spinning off NBCUniversal and Sky into a standalone media and entertainment business while the original Comcast retains its broadband and wireless operations. The separation is expected to take about a year, with co-CEO Mike Cavanagh leading NBCUniversal and former CFO Michael Angelakis becoming Comcast CEO, as the media arm faces streaming and consolidation pressure.

title: Mozilla warns of indirect prompt injection risk in AI coding agents
blurb: Mozilla's 0DIN security team demonstrated an indirect prompt injection attack in which a malicious GitHub repository can compromise a developer's machine through AI coding agents like Claude Code, without containing any malicious code itself. The repo's setup instructions steer the agent into running an initialization command that fetches a reverse-shell payload from an attacker-controlled DNS TXT record at runtime, leaving it invisible to code review, static analysis, and the agent.

title: Academic papers and conference materials offer a deep dive into China's all-CPU LineShine, which pairs custom 304-core Arm CPUs with HBM to top the Top500 (Timothy Prickett Morgan/The Next Platform)
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer topped the Top500 using an all-CPU design that pairs custom 304-core Arm processors with HBM memory, as detailed in newly available academic papers and conference materials. The architecture forgoes GPUs entirely, relying on high core counts and high-bandwidth memory to reach leading performance.

title: GPT-5.6 gets better at cybersecurity
blurb: OpenAI started rolling out its GPT-5.6 series in limited preview to trusted partners via the API and Codex, led by the flagship Sol model alongside the balanced Terra and the faster, cheaper Luna. Sol tops the Terminal-Bench 2.1 coding benchmark and advances long-horizon cybersecurity tasks like vulnerability research, though OpenAI says it could not execute a complete cyberattack on its own in testing.

title: China’s hollow-core fiber trial pushes 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration — milestone targets AI-era networking bottlenecks
blurb: YOFC field-trialed hollow-core fiber hitting 51.3 Tb/s over 128 miles without signal regeneration, targeting AI-era networking bottlenecks.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic Limit New AI Models to Trump-Approved Customers During Cybersecurity Review
blurb: OpenAI and Anthropic restricted their newest AI models to a small set of government-approved partners while the Trump administration vets them for cybersecurity risks. OpenAI limited GPT-5.6 Sol to approved customers and called the process temporary, while Anthropic redeployed its Mythos 5 model to select cyber defenders two weeks after the Commerce Department effectively banned it.

title: Ex-Anthropic researchers raise $200M for self-improving AI
blurb: Mirendil, founded by two researchers who left Anthropic after about a year, raised $200M at a $1B valuation in one of the sector's largest seed rounds, co-led by Andreessen Horowitz and Kleiner Perkins with Nvidia participating. The startup plans to build AI that does the work of an AI researcher and sell it to outside organizations, the self-improving loop that frontier labs run in-house but bar customers from replicating.

title: Sources: CXMT and Tencent sign a ~$3B, three-year DRAM supply agreement for servers ahead of CXMT's IPO; CXMT is in talks with other major Chinese companies (Reuters)
blurb: CXMT and Tencent signed a roughly $3B, three-year DRAM server supply deal ahead of CXMT's IPO.

title: AWS raises GPU prices 20% as the memory crunch bites
blurb: AWS raised prices for EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML by roughly 20% starting in July, the second increase in six months after a 15% rise in January. The hike hits only the reserved-GPU option used by teams training large models, sparing Amazon's in-house Trainium chip, and reflects a high-bandwidth memory shortage that has made GPU capacity the binding constraint on AI.

title: OpenAI hires Apple’s Vision Pro chief Paul Meade
blurb: OpenAI hired Paul Meade, the Apple vice president who led Vision Pro hardware engineering for seven years and ran its smart glasses program, to work on its planned family of AI-powered devices. Meade rejoins former Apple colleagues Jony Ive, Tang Tan, and Evans Hankey at OpenAI, making him the most senior Apple defection yet in the AI hardware talent war.

title: Beyond automation: How much does AI really cost?
blurb: Enterprise AI costs are spiraling not from high-token executive use but from quiet, high-frequency automated background tasks, with one anonymous company spending $500M in a single month on Claude after rolling it out without usage limits. The author argues operational cost is the product of three variables (tokens × frequency × users) that must be modeled per workflow before the architecture is finalized, since deployments that avoided budget surprises all did this upfront.

title: OpenAI may delay its IPO to 2027 to hold out for $1tn
blurb: OpenAI is weighing a public listing as late as 2027, with Altman reportedly rejecting an earlier debut while holding out for a $1tn valuation, according to Bloomberg. The delay sent SoftBank shares down as much as 13% and risks letting Anthropic, which has also filed, go public first.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture told some staff to stop using AI for basic tasks like converting PDFs to slides, citing a rapid escalation in token spend that has made costs unpredictable. The consultancy's agentic AI strategy lead said C-level executives are still questioning whether the spending delivers value, and framed the problem as one every AI-using enterprise will face, opening a services opportunity for Accenture to advise clients on it.

title: DeepSeek’s $7.4bn raise is one symptom of China’s AI cash surge
blurb: DeepSeek closed a $7.4bn round at a valuation above $50bn, the largest first-time raise by a Chinese startup and the lab's first outside funding after three years on founder Liang Wenfeng's own wealth. The raise is part of a broader surge that has China hosting 381 unicorns and minting a new one every five days, with US export curbs increasingly acting as a forcing function for domestic AI spending.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix at Center of South Korea's AI Drive
blurb: South Korea is coordinating at least $880B in investments from companies including Samsung Electronics and SK Hynix to build AI infrastructure it has called essential to competing in the AI era. The push positions the country's two largest chipmakers, both major suppliers of memory used in AI systems, at the center of a national effort to expand domestic compute capacity.

title: Herdr: Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal
blurb: Herdr is an open-source terminal multiplexer for AI coding agents, organizing them into workspaces, tabs, and panes with mouse-native click, drag, and split controls. Agents keep running after the client detaches and can be reattached from another terminal, and each pane shows the agent's real terminal output rather than a GUI reinterpretation.

title: Chinese robotics startup AI² Robotics raised ~$736M at a ~$2.9B valuation; Alibaba-backed X Square Robot raised an undisclosed amount at a ~$2.9B valuation (Bloomberg)
blurb: Two Chinese robotics startups raised funding at roughly $2.9B valuations, with AI² Robotics securing about $736M and Alibaba-backed X Square Robot raising an undisclosed sum. The matching valuations underscore intensifying investor competition in China's humanoid and embodied-AI sector, where multiple well-capitalized players are now racing to commercialize robotics.

title: Google Cloud to Offer Specialist AI Models for Science Research
blurb: Google will begin offering SandboxAQ's specialist AI models through Google Cloud, expanding enterprise and research access to systems built for drug discovery, materials science, and semiconductor manufacturing. The deal extends Google's cloud catalog beyond general-purpose models into domain-specific tools aimed at accelerating scientific research workloads.

title: Sources: disconnected US military databases may have led to the February 28 strike on an Iranian school; some see AI as a fix, others fear it amplifies errors (Katrina Manson/Los Angeles Times)
blurb: Disconnected US military databases may have contributed to the February 28 strike on an Iranian school, according to sources cited by the Los Angeles Times. Some officials view AI as a way to stitch together fragmented intelligence systems, while others warn it could amplify the same data errors that lead to targeting mistakes.

title: China Pushes All School Levels to Teach AI in Xi’s Tech Drive
blurb: China announced a formal plan to integrate AI instruction across all levels of its school system, from primary grades through university. The initiative ties the national education curriculum to Xi Jinping's campaign to lead in advanced technologies, building a domestic talent pipeline as the country competes with the US for AI dominance.

title: CacheWeaver Reorders RAG Evidence for Prefix-Cache Reuse: Prefix-Cache-Aware Evidence Reordering
blurb: CacheWeaver is a prompt-layer method that reorders retrieved RAG chunks so a serving engine can reuse more of its KV prefix cache, requiring no changes to the inference engine. Because time-to-first-token in retrieval-augmented serving is dominated by prefilling the evidence, re-sequencing chunks to maximize the shared opening prefix lets the engine skip that work and emit its first token sooner than relevance-ordered caching allows.

title: Google reportedly capped Meta's use of Gemini AI for coding and chatbots
blurb: Google capped Meta's access to its Gemini AI models after Meta exceeded its allotted computing capacity, according to The Financial Times. Meta, which lacks its own cloud business, had turned to Gemini for customer service, advertiser chatbots, and coding because it outperformed the company's own Llama models, and has now asked employees to use tokens more efficiently.

title: Crunchbase Data: Q2 Brought The Most Billion-Dollar Startup Exits Since 2021
blurb: Big startup exits are back, but the story is size, not count. Q2 2026 brought the most billion-dollar venture-backed exits since the 2021 peak, led by SpaceX's record IPO, which hit a $2.1 trillion first-day market cap and raised $75 billion, followed days later by its $60 billion acquisition of Cursor, the priciest purchase of a private startup ever. Deal counts are still well below the 2021 boom, but returns blow past it, and with Anthropic and OpenAI filing confidentially for IPOs that could test the trillion-dollar mark, the size trend has room to run.

title: Ex-Anthropic researchers raise $200M for self-improving AI
blurb: Two ex-Anthropic researchers raised $200M at a $1B valuation for Mirendil, one of the sector's largest seed rounds, to sell the self-improving AI that frontier labs build for themselves and bar customers from copying. The labs run AI on the problem of building AI in-house, then use their terms of service to stop outsiders doing the same. Mirendil's bet is to package that researcher-replacing engine as a platform any organization can point at its own problems, compressing months of model-building into days for teams without a machine-learning bench.

title: OpenAI may delay its IPO to 2027 to hold out for $1tn
blurb: OpenAI is weighing an IPO as late as 2027, holding out for a $1 trillion valuation after Altman reportedly rejected an earlier debut at a lower price, per Bloomberg. The discipline has a cost others are paying: SoftBank shares fell as much as 13% on the news, and Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs slipped on the prospect of delayed fees, while Anthropic has already filed and could ring the bell first. Altman is betting the prize grows faster than the patience runs out, and a shaky AI trade just reminded everyone the bet is not free.

title: Samsung, SK Hynix to Spend $520 Billion on Chip Plants in South Korea
blurb: Samsung and SK Hynix said they will spend $520B building chip plants in South Korea, after concluding their existing investments wouldn't keep pace with demand. The two largest memory-chip makers are responding to a worldwide shortage that current capacity can't cover. For founders building on AI, the constraint is moving upstream: memory and compute supply, not model quality, is increasingly what sets the ceiling on what you can ship and what it costs.

title: AWS raises GPU prices 20% as the memory crunch bites
blurb: AWS raised prices on EC2 Capacity Blocks for ML, the reserved Nvidia GPU tier serious AI teams use for training, by roughly 20% starting in July, its second hike in six months after a 15% increase in January. The squeeze traces to high-bandwidth memory, which takes years to build, so less memory means fewer GPUs and fewer data centers. That scarcity hands AWS, Microsoft, Google, and Oracle pricing power, and the higher cost ripples straight through to the startups and enterprises renting the priciest tier of compute.

title: Beyond automation: How much does AI really cost?
blurb: The hidden line item in AI budgets isn't your power users, it's the high-frequency automated tasks running quietly in the background. One enterprise spent $500M on Claude in a single month because nobody set usage limits before rollout, Uber burned its entire 2026 AI budget before midyear, and JPMorgan published a report titled "AI Token Costs Are Eating into Internet Profits." The teams that avoided the surprise modeled token cost per workflow before finalizing architecture, treating cost as tokens times frequency times users rather than a function of how sophisticated the task looks.

title: Peec AI doubles its valuation betting GEO is the new SEO
blurb: Peec AI is in talks to raise around $10M at a $200M pre-money valuation, roughly double its worth eight months ago, on a bet that generative engine optimization replaces SEO. Instead of tracking Google rankings, Peec tracks whether brands like Chanel and TUI show up when shoppers ask ChatGPT or Perplexity for the best product, and it scrapes the screen rather than querying APIs to see what real users see. The risk is the moat: rivals like Profound and Semrush already generate and optimize content, not just monitor it, and if measurement becomes a feature, $200M looks steep.

title: China Births Two Robot Unicorns as Sector Funding Stays Strong
blurb: China minted two new robotics unicorns this week, each valued above $2.9B, as funding into the sector holds steady despite the broader venture slowdown. The money is flowing toward humanoid and industrial robotics players positioned to challenge Tesla and Figure AI, signaling that Beijing's hardware bet has investor conviction behind it. For founders, the takeaway is that the robotics race is now a two-front war, with Chinese capital backing domestic challengers at a pace that mirrors the US frontier.

title: DeepSeek’s $7.4bn raise is one symptom of China’s AI cash surge
blurb: US export curbs and model bans were meant to slow China's AI push, but the money is moving the other way. DeepSeek closed a $7.4bn round at a valuation above $50bn, its first outside capital after three years on the founder's own wealth, reportedly to fund a war chest big enough to match Anthropic's Mythos. The surge runs wider: China now has 381 unicorns, mints a new one every five days, and is pouring at least 46bn yuan into humanoid robots this year. Each new restriction has become a forcing function, and the gap keeps closing.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture has told staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks like converting PDFs to slides, after internal meetings flagged "rapid escalation in AI token spend." The shift to consumption-based pricing, combined with employees "tokenmaxxing" to prove their AI uptake, has pushed spend to an inflection point where C-level execs are openly questioning the return. Accenture's agentic AI lead says this isn't niche, every AI-using enterprise will hit it, which is why the consultancy now plans to sell clients the service of untangling their own token bills.

title: Comcast Plans to Spin Off NBCUniversal Into New Media Firm
blurb: Comcast plans to spin off NBCUniversal and Sky into a separate publicly traded media company, splitting its content businesses from cable-TV and internet. The move follows the same logic playing out across legacy media, where shrinking linear-TV assets get carved off so the faster-growing connectivity business can be valued on its own terms. For founders, it is another signal that the bundle era is unwinding and conglomerates are betting that two focused companies are worth more than one diversified giant.

title: SpaceX's $25 billion bond sale drives huge demand - and a potential headache for investors
blurb: SpaceX raised $25B in senior unsecured notes last week, pulling nearly $90B in orders just two weeks after the largest IPO in history. The catch is that owning both SPCX equity and SpaceX bonds isn't diversification, it's the same execution risk priced into two instruments, with both the equity story and the debt service riding on Starlink scaling and Starship working. Equity investors took the hint, selling the stock down more than 13% on the week against a $5B net loss and capex that more than doubled year over year.

title: Chinese robotics startup AI² Robotics raised ~$736M at a ~$2.9B valuation; Alibaba-backed X Square Robot raised an undisclosed amount at a ~$2.9B valuation (Bloomberg)
blurb: Two Chinese embodied-AI startups hit roughly $2.9B valuations on the same day, with AI² Robotics raising about $736M and Alibaba-backed X Square Robot raising an undisclosed sum. China's humanoid and robotics sector is now drawing nine-figure rounds at multibillion-dollar prices while most Western robotics startups remain a stage behind. The capital is concentrating fast, and the labor-automation race increasingly looks like a state-backed Chinese push rather than a Silicon Valley one.

title: AirTrunk Is Said to Be Near Confidential Filing for Biggest Singapore IPO in Years
blurb: Blackstone-backed AirTrunk is close to a confidential filing for a Singapore REIT IPO that would be the city-state's largest in years. The listing turns a privately held hyperscale data center operator into public infrastructure stock, giving investors a direct way to buy the physical buildout behind the AI compute boom. For founders, it is another sign that the capital crowding into AI has moved past models and chips into the power, land, and real estate that everything else depends on.

title: An interview with Axon CEO Rick Smith, who transformed the Taser maker into a policing software company, as its revenue from AI policing tools rises 700%+ YoY (Victoria Albert/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Axon, the company that made its name selling Tasers, now makes most of its growth from policing software, and its AI tools are pulling revenue up more than 700% year over year. CEO Rick Smith spent a decade turning a hardware vendor into a recurring-software business that sits inside police workflows, from body cameras to report-writing. The pattern is the one founders keep seeing: the durable moat isn't the device, it's owning the data and the daily workflow the customer can't easily leave.

title: SpaceX wants its own gas pipeline to fuel Starship
blurb: SpaceX filed in Texas to build Starpipe, an eight-mile natural gas pipeline feeding liquid methane to Starship at its Starbase launch site, with construction possibly starting next month. The logic is throughput: each launch burns about 630,000 gallons of methane currently trucked in by hundreds of tankers, a pace that can't support Musk's plan to scale from 12 test flights to thousands of launches a year. The pipeline is the visible edge of deeper vertical integration, with SpaceX signing more than 100 oil and gas leases since 2023 and exploring its own drilling.

title: Comcast is splitting in two
blurb: Comcast will split into two publicly traded companies, spinning its NBCUniversal and Sky media assets out from the broadband and wireless business that keeps the Comcast name. The move walls off the profitable connectivity unit from a media arm under pressure from consolidation and streaming, with each company getting its own CEO and Comcast shareholders holding stock in both. The separation is expected to take about a year, and it reflects a broader pattern of cable conglomerates cutting their declining TV businesses loose to protect the parts still growing.

title: Trump threatens 100% tariffs over digital services taxes
blurb: Trump posted on Truth Social that any country imposing a digital services tax on US firms will face a 100% tariff on all goods, language he says would supersede trade deals, including the EU's just-approved Turnberry deal that caps most tariffs at 15%. France, Italy, Spain, and the UK already levy DSTs on Google, Apple, Amazon, and Meta, and Brussels called taxation its sovereign right and vowed to respond. The catch is that the Supreme Court struck down his reciprocal tariffs this year, so it is unclear what legal tool lets him impose a sweeping 100% levy without Congress.

title: From charging EVs and drones to powering data centres: London-based Gaussion raises €24.5 million
blurb: Gaussion, a UCL spinout, raised €24.5M co-led by BGF and AlbionVC to scale its magnetic charging tech that speeds up battery charging and extends pack life without touching the underlying chemistry. The system retrofits onto any lithium-ion battery and spans automotive, aerospace, grid storage, and data centre power, with 66 patent applications across 17 families already filed. The bet is that the battery, not the chip, becomes the binding constraint on AI compute, drones, and EVs, and the companies that move the physics ceiling capture the value others assume sits in silicon.

title: Amid rising UK cyber talent growth, Osney Capital closes oversubscribed €69 million debut fund
blurb: Specialist capital keeps winning in a tight fundraising market. Osney Capital closed its debut fund oversubscribed at its €69M hard cap, backed by the British Business Bank, Imperial College London's endowment, and accreditation from the UK's national security investment fund, making it one of the country's first dedicated cybersecurity Seed funds. The thesis is sovereign cyber capability, as geopolitical tension pushes governments and industry toward home-grown defense. Osney has already written seven pre-Seed and Seed checks between €289k and €2.8M, against a target of 30 companies.

title: Rocket Lab Buys Satellite Operator Iridium in Bid to Challenge SpaceX
blurb: Rocket Lab acquired satellite operator Iridium, giving it a working orbital fleet and the wireless spectrum to start competing with SpaceX head-on. The move turns a launch provider into a vertically integrated space company, owning the rocket, the satellites, and the connectivity rather than just selling rides to orbit. Owning spectrum is the part that matters, because that is the scarce asset Starlink built its lead on, and it is the one thing money alone cannot buy fast.

title: Microsoft’s $570 Billion Rout Sets Up Its Worst Month Since 2000
blurb: Microsoft has shed about $570 billion in market value this month, putting it on track for its worst monthly drop since the dot-com crash in 2000. The selloff isn't about a bad quarter, it's about doubt over whether the software giant can defend its position as AI reshapes how software gets built and bought. When the market starts pricing an incumbent's moat as a liability rather than a fortress, it signals that even the safest names in tech are now being repriced for the AI transition.

title: Verizon, BT Merge International Units in Pivot to Home Markets
blurb: Verizon and BT agreed to fold their international units into a joint venture, stripping the low-margin businesses off both balance sheets so each carrier can focus on its home market. The deal signals where mature telecom is heading: out of the sprawling global footprints built up over decades and back toward the dense, defensible territory each company actually owns. For founders selling into carriers, the buyer is narrowing its surface area, and the international edge cases that used to be a sales path are now the first thing getting cut.

title: ‘DirtyClone’ Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
blurb: JFrog published technical details and a proof of concept for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation bug that lets any local user gain root by corrupting file-backed page-cache memory through zero-copy socket buffer paths. The fix landed in v7.1-rc5, but only kernels carrying the full DirtyFrag patch chain are protected, leaving Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu systems with unprivileged user namespaces exposed.

title: Mozilla warns of indirect prompt injection risk in AI coding agents
blurb: Mozilla's 0DIN team showed that a malicious GitHub repo can compromise a developer's machine via indirect prompt injection, with no malicious code in the repo itself. The proof-of-concept tricks agents like Claude Code into running setup steps that pipe an attacker-controlled DNS TXT record to bash, fetching a reverse shell at runtime that's invisible to code review, static analysis, and the agent reading the repo.

title: Google's new hand-wave reCAPTCHA can be bypassed with a stock photo
blurb: Google's new hand-wave reCAPTCHA, which extracts 21 hand-knuckle coordinates from camera video, can be passed by feeding a stock image through OBS Virtual Camera.

title: OTel and mesh-derived metrics: A 2026 reference
blurb: This reference shows how Linkerd's sidecar proxy emits golden metrics for every inbound and outbound request the moment a workload is meshed, with no instrumentation, SDK calls, or image rebuilds, giving you the east-west traffic that application-layer OpenTelemetry never sees. It walks through where mesh metrics overlap with OTel and where they don't, then wires the proxy's Prometheus endpoint into an existing OTel Collector pipeline so both layers land in the same backend.

title: Herdr: Agent multiplexer that lives in your terminal
blurb: Herdr is a terminal-based multiplexer for managing multiple coding agents, using workspaces, tabs, and panes that you control with the mouse or tmux-style keybindings. Agents keep running after you detach and reattach, and unlike Electron or native GUI wrappers, you interact with each agent's real terminal process rather than a rewritten view of it.

title: CacheWeaver Reorders RAG Evidence for Prefix-Cache Reuse: Prefix-Cache-Aware Evidence Reordering
blurb: CacheWeaver re-sequences retrieved RAG chunks so a prompt's opening tokens match what's already in the serving engine's KV prefix cache, letting it skip prefill work that naive relevance-ordered retrieval forces it to redo. The method lives entirely at the prompt layer, requires no change to engines like SGLang or vLLM, and cuts time-to-first-token by recovering prefix-cache reuse that relevance ordering leaves on the table.

title: Dissecting Apple's Sparse Image Format (ASIF)
blurb: The author reverse-engineers ASIF, the sparse disk image format Apple introduced in macOS Tahoe for its Virtualization framework, walking through the process of writing a parser from scratch. The post starts with eyeballing hexdumps and a numbered test pattern, then decodes the file magic and big-endian header fields, with research notes throughout aimed at readers new to reverse-engineering binary formats.

title: Pong game recompiles its own source code every frame — winning entry at IOCCC29 was generated by a custom compiler
blurb: An IOCCC29-winning Pong recompiles its own source every frame, each run printing the C for the next frame with state baked in.

title: The Python exception that leaked tenant secrets
blurb: A production incident leaked tenant config secrets into plaintext logs because a dataclass with `api_key` and `webhook_secret` fields was interpolated into an exception message, and the auto-generated `__repr__` serialized every field verbatim into the traceback. The author shows that `raise e` was only the symptom; the real fix is deciding what a config object is allowed to expose in its repr before it ever reaches `logger.exception()`.

title: No, you haven't accidentally downloaded a banking app – that really is the new Winter Olympics 2030 logo
blurb: Alpes 2030 revealed the Winter Olympics identity, a mark of radiant lines converging into an alpine peak rendered in ice-blue and alpenglow pink gradients, with the Paralympic version inverting the same shape into a connected system. The execution is clean and scales from venue signage to avatars, though its geometric minimalism reads closer to a fintech brand than a winter sports event.

title: Mozilla warns of indirect prompt injection risk in AI coding agents
blurb: Mozilla's 0DIN team demonstrated an indirect prompt injection attack in which a malicious GitHub repo steers AI coding agents like Claude Code into running setup commands that fetch a reverse-shell payload at runtime via a DNS TXT record, leaving nothing malicious in the repo for code review or static analysis to catch. Treat setup instructions in unfamiliar repos as untrusted code regardless of agent recommendations, and design agents to surface what a command actually executes rather than its literal string.

title: ‘DirtyClone’ Linux Kernel Vulnerability Leads to Root Access
blurb: JFrog published technical details and a PoC for DirtyClone (CVE-2026-43503, CVSS 8.8), a Linux kernel local privilege escalation flaw in the networking stack's socket-buffer handling that lets any local user gain root. The bug is a variant of the DirtyFrag/Fragnesia family affecting Debian, Fedora, and Ubuntu with unprivileged user namespaces enabled; only kernels updated to v7.1-rc5 with the complete fix chain are protected, so partially patched LTS branches remain exposed.

title: OpenAI and Anthropic Limit New AI Models to Trump-Approved Customers During Cybersecurity Review
blurb: OpenAI and Anthropic restricted their newest AI models to government-approved customers during a Trump administration cybersecurity review, with OpenAI limiting GPT-5.6 Sol to vetted partners and Anthropic redeploying its Mythos 5 model to a small group of cyber defenders after a Commerce Department ban. The vetting follows a June executive order letting the government assess national-security risks of frontier models for up to 30 days before public release.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions tied to a single threat actor it tracks as StegoAd, which used steganography to hide payloads inside PNG, WebP, and WOFF2 image and font files, then activated days post-install to steal credentials and run ad fraud. Active since at least 2021, the extensions posed as ad blockers, VPNs, and translators across an install base of up to 2.6 million, using server-side validation, DevTools detection, and execution gates to evade analysis.

title: GPT-5.6 gets better at cybersecurity
blurb: OpenAI began rolling out its GPT-5.6 model series (Sol, Terra, and Luna) in limited preview to trusted partners via API and Codex, coordinating the release with the U.S. government before wider availability. Sol advances long-horizon security tasks like vulnerability research and exploitation but could not complete a full cyberattack autonomously in OpenAI's tests, which paired the model with a layered safety stack that refuses disguised offensive cyber and biology requests.

title: US Offers $10 Million Bounty for Russian State Hackers as Messaging App Attacks Evolve
blurb: The US State Department is offering up to $10M for information on UNC5792 and UNC4221, Russian intelligence-linked actors phishing officials, military leaders, and Ukrainian targets to hijack Signal and WhatsApp accounts. A fresh CISA/FBI update warns the actors now request victims' Backup Recovery Keys, which stay valid across re-registration on the same number, so compromised users must generate a new key to revoke access.

title: Google's new hand-wave reCAPTCHA can be bypassed with a stock photo
blurb: Google's experimental hand-gesture reCAPTCHA, which asks users to wave at their camera and extracts 21 hand-knuckle coordinates via AI to verify humanness, can be defeated with a static stock photo of a hand fed through OBS Virtual Camera. Researchers reproduced the bypass across multiple still images and note it requires no AI bot or video, so a simple Python script can automate it end to end.

title: Microsoft Edge gets tons of security features, including AI model that can see your screen
blurb: Microsoft detailed new Edge for Business security controls, including DLP policies that block copy-paste and downloads from unmanaged locations, contractor file isolation to OneDrive, extension installation governance, and native Defender and Purview integration. Edge also added an on-device computer-vision model that watches the screen to block scareware and malicious content in real time without relying on site reputation.

title: US seizes hundreds of FIFA World Cup illegal streaming domains
blurb: The DOJ Criminal Division seized nearly 400 domains streaming 2026 FIFA World Cup matches without authorization, in a coordinated takedown dubbed Operation Offsides spanning Peru, Bulgaria, Croatia, Romania, Poland, and Colombia. Beyond copyright enforcement, officials flagged that illegal sports-streaming sites routinely expose visitors to malware and credential theft, a vector worth noting as the tournament drives traffic to lookalike and pirate domains.

title: What the post-quantum executive order really demands of CISOs
blurb: A new executive order sets binding post-quantum deadlines: federal high-value systems must move key establishment to PQC by Dec. 31, 2030, and digital signatures by Dec. 31, 2031. The author argues CISOs should treat this as a readiness and ownership problem, not a future compliance checkbox, since "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" collection of long-lived data is already operational and procurement and migration cycles put 2030 inside current planning horizons.

title: Robot Police Officers
blurb: The Sacramento County Sheriff's Office deployed a drone equipped with a high-powered magnet to disarm a barricaded suspect, using it to pull a knife from the person's hand inside a residence after negotiations failed. The June 22 incident marks an early operational use of remote robotics for direct physical intervention in police standoffs, raising open questions about use-of-force policy and oversight as the tactic spreads.

title: 'Chinese Mythos' adds further fuel to sovereignty debate in AI security
blurb: Qihoo 360, the Chinese firm placed on the U.S. Entity List in 2020, claimed to have built Tulongfeng and Yitianzhen, with CEO Hongyi Zhou pitching the latter as "China's version of Mythos," Anthropic's automated vulnerability researcher. Zhou said Tulongfeng has surfaced 3,432 flaws across open-source, binary, and agentic systems, though the figures are unverified and trail Mythos partners' 10,000-plus confirmed findings under Project Glasswing.

title: FCC escalates China crackdown by closing equipment import loophole
blurb: The FCC moved to bar imports of Chinese-made network equipment that received authorization before 2022, closing a loophole the agency says could have let Covered List vendors like Huawei and ZTE maintain access to U.S. critical infrastructure. The rules take effect in July and extend Chairman Brendan Carr's China crackdown, which already covers foreign-made routers, blanket carrier authorizations, and subsea cable supply chains following the Salt Typhoon intrusions.

title: Gamaredon Expands Ukraine Attacks with New Malware and Cloud Service Abuse
blurb: ESET reported that Russian APT Gamaredon ran 35 spear-phishing campaigns against Ukrainian government and military targets in 2025, deploying six new PowerShell tools and weaponizing a patched WinRAR flaw (CVE-2025-8088) to drop HTA downloaders into the Startup folder for persistence. The group increasingly routed command-and-control through tunnel services and serverless worker platforms to mask its back-end infrastructure, so defenders should monitor outbound traffic to these providers and patch WinRAR.

title: Beyond automation: How much does AI really cost?
blurb: A CIO.com analysis argues enterprise AI cost overruns come not from senior staff running high-token sessions but from quiet, high-frequency automated background tasks, citing one firm that spent $500M in a single month on Claude after rolling it out without usage limits. The author models total cost as tokens × frequency × users and urges teams to size token volume per workflow before finalizing architecture, the step that separated budget-surprise deployments from controlled ones.

title: Google Cloud to Offer Specialist AI Models for Science Research
blurb: Google Cloud will offer SandboxAQ's specialist AI models for drug discovery, materials science, and semiconductor manufacturing through its cloud platform. The deal gives enterprise and research customers access to domain-specific scientific models without a separate vendor relationship, while extending SandboxAQ's distribution to Google Cloud's existing install base.

title: Microsoft Removes 119 Edge Extensions That Hid Malware in Images and Fonts
blurb: Microsoft removed 119 malicious Edge extensions tied to a single threat actor it calls StegoAd, which hid payloads inside PNG, WebP, and WOFF2 files using steganography to evade scanners before stealing credentials and running ad fraud. The campaign had been live since 2021 across a 2.6 million install base, exposing how thinly the Edge Add-ons store vets the ad blockers, VPNs, and translators enterprise users sideload onto managed browsers.

title: ‘What we’re seeing right now is just rapid escalation in AI token spend’: Accenture tells staff to stop using AI for unnecessary tasks amid surging costs
blurb: Accenture told some staff to stop using AI for basic tasks like converting PDFs to slides, after leaked internal meeting audio revealed "rapid escalation in AI token spend" that has executives questioning the return on their AI investment. The consultancy's agentic AI strategy lead framed unpredictable token costs as an industry-wide inflection point, one Accenture now plans to sell client services around as providers like Anthropic and GitHub shift to consumption-based pricing.

title: Musk approved to acquire optical transceiver developer taking on Chinese dominance
blurb: Elon Musk received FTC fast-track antitrust approval to acquire Mesh Optical Technologies, a SpaceX-engineer-founded startup whose Alpha C1 transceiver moves 1.6 Tb/s at one-third the power of rivals by dropping retimer DSP silicon. The deal aims to challenge Chinese dominance in optical interconnects and secure a domestic, mass-producible supply for the AI clusters and Starlink-style space infrastructure across Musk's holdings.

title: Microsoft Edge gets tons of security features, including AI model that can see your screen
blurb: Microsoft detailed new security features for Edge for Business, including shadow-AI controls that redirect employees to sanctioned tools like Microsoft 365 Copilot via Purview DLP, contractor file-download restrictions, and an on-device computer-vision model that watches the screen to block scareware regardless of site reputation. The features lean on native Defender and Purview integration, deepening the case for Edge in Entra-standardized shops while adding PAYG licensing costs for capabilities like shadow-AI redirection.

title: Apple may struggle to get clearance for Chinese RAM, even for Chinese iPhones
blurb: Apple is lobbying the Trump administration for clearance to buy NAND and DRAM from blacklisted Chinese suppliers CXMT and YMTC, even for iPhones sold only in China. The bid faces long odds, since acting National Security Advisor Marco Rubio co-signed a 2022 Senate letter opposing the same YMTC sourcing on supply-chain security grounds, leaving Apple's component diversification strategy exposed to federal-use bans.

title: What the post-quantum executive order really demands of CISOs
blurb: The author argues the new post-quantum executive order reframes PQC as a readiness and ownership problem for CISOs, not a future cryptography exercise. Federal high-value systems must move key establishment to PQC by Dec. 31, 2030 and digital signatures by Dec. 31, 2031, deadlines that already sit inside enterprise procurement and architecture-review horizons. The more urgent risk is "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" collection, which means long-lived sensitive data is functionally exposed today regardless of the transition timeline.

title: US seizes hundreds of FIFA World Cup illegal streaming domains
blurb: The US Justice Department seized nearly 400 domains streaming 2026 FIFA World Cup matches illegally, in a coordinated takedown dubbed Operation Offsides spanning Peru, Bulgaria, Croatia, Romania, Poland, and Colombia. The action drew leads from FIFA, NBCUniversal, beIN Media Group, and Warner Bros., and follows a separate shutdown of 44 PirloTV-linked piracy domains that drew over 950 million visits a year.

title: AI sprawl, token consumption ratchet up tech overspending
blurb: A Flexera survey of 500+ IT asset managers found more than two-thirds lack accurate visibility into AI software usage, and roughly 3 in 5 reported year-over-year AI overspend as wasted SaaS spend climbed 10 percentage points. The report names "tokenmaxxing," the practice of rewarding consumption without governing value, as a driver, citing Uber's burn through its entire 2026 AI budget in four months.

title: 'Chinese Mythos' adds further fuel to sovereignty debate in AI security
blurb: Qihoo 360, a Chinese firm on the U.S. Entity List, released Yitianzhen and Tulongfeng, vulnerability-research models its CEO framed as China's sovereign answer to Anthropic's Mythos. The pitch leans on AI sovereignty rather than raw capability, with founder Hongyi Zhou conceding Chinese models trail U.S. frontier systems by 20% to 30% and claiming 3,432 flaws found, a figure Reuters notes is unverified.

title: FCC escalates China crackdown by closing equipment import loophole
blurb: The FCC moved to bar pre-2022-authorized network equipment from Covered List vendors like Huawei and ZTE, closing a loophole that let barred gear remain in U.S. critical infrastructure and public-safety systems. The rules take effect in July and extend Chairman Brendan Carr's China crackdown, which already covers blanket carrier authorizations, foreign-made routers, and subsea cable provisions.

title: Huawei autonomous networks framework canonized for telco agent proliferation
blurb: LF Networking formalized OpenAN, an open-source autonomous-networks framework first developed by Huawei and China Mobile, with Orange, ZTE, AIS, Infosys, and AsiaInfo on board. The project builds on Huawei's A2A-T protocol, which extends Google's A2A standard for telecom-specific multi-agent collaboration across OSS and EMS layers, targeting TM Forum Level 5 autonomy with scenario solution packages due at December's TM Forum event.

title: etcd-operator joins Cozystack with a new v1alpha2 API
blurb: The etcd-operator project was donated to Cozystack and re-implemented from scratch under a new etcd-operator.cozystack.io/v1alpha2 API, dropping the StatefulSet model in favor of driving etcd's native Membership API for MemberAdd, MemberPromote, and MemberRemove directly. The rewrite reconciles each member's Pod and PVC independently and adds learner-mode members one at a time, giving the operator full control over quorum changes during scaling and graceful removal.

title: OTel and mesh-derived metrics: A 2026 reference
blurb: This CNCF post explains how to combine Linkerd's mesh-derived metrics with an existing OpenTelemetry pipeline, covering the east-west service traffic that application-layer instrumentation misses. Once a workload is meshed, the Linkerd proxy emits golden metrics for every inbound and outbound request on port 4191, with no SDK calls or image rebuilds. The reference shows where mesh metrics overlap with OTel and where they don't, plus a working Collector config and Grafana dashboard to land both layers in the same backend.

title: Peec AI doubles its valuation betting GEO is the new SEO
blurb: Peec AI is in talks to raise roughly $10M at a $200M pre-money valuation, about double its worth eight months ago, betting that generative engine optimization replaces SEO as AI answers drain clicks from Google's blue links. The Berlin startup tracks whether brands like Chanel and TUI surface in ChatGPT and Perplexity buying prompts, but rivals Profound and Semrush already generate optimized content rather than just monitoring visibility.

title: What is AI search optimization? (& why marketers should care)
blurb: AI search optimization, which HubSpot calls answer engine optimization, is the practice of making a brand more likely to be cited by ChatGPT, Perplexity, Gemini, and AI Overviews. The traffic is still small but converts well above search: across 1,200-plus publisher sites in a Microsoft Clarity study, AI-referred visitors signed up at roughly 11 times the rate of search visitors, and AI traffic grew 66% in 2025.

title: Adobe: online spending across all retailers in the US hit $26.4B during Amazon's four-day Prime Day event, up 9.3% YoY; Walmart and Target also hosted sales (Spencer Soper/Bloomberg)
blurb: US online spending across all retailers reached $26.4B during Amazon's four-day Prime Day event, up 9.3% YoY, per Adobe, with Walmart and Target running competing sales. The expanded promo window pulls discretionary demand into a single July period, raising CPMs and CAC for any brand bidding on retail-media or paid-social during the event and rewarding teams that front-load creative and budget.

title: BIS warns stablecoins are more like ETFs than actual money, and they're creating FX risk
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements argued in its annual report that stablecoins function more like ETF shares than money, citing prices that deviate from par and redemptions that aren't guaranteed instant or at face value. The report also warned that dollar-pegged stablecoins are accelerating dollarization in vulnerable economies, undermining local currencies and routing around traditional capital controls.

title: India’s USDT premium tops 8.5% as crypto remittance crackdown squeezes stablecoin supply: report
blurb: India's USDT premium climbed above 8.5%, more than double its typical 3-4% range, after Enforcement Directorate raids on Bengaluru crypto remittance firms disrupted the domestic stablecoin supply pipeline. The ED alleges the firms moved over $265M in unauthorized cross-border transfers using USDT to bypass FEMA channels, with market makers pulling back on overseas USDT purchases ahead of a July 2 parliamentary review of virtual digital asset rules.

title: Galaxy Research cuts Clarity Act 2026 passage odds to 50%, citing Senate calendar concerns
blurb: Galaxy Research cut its odds of the Clarity Act becoming law in 2026 to 50%, down from 60% earlier this month, citing a tightening Senate calendar and stalled negotiations rather than the bill's substance. With no floor date or motion to proceed scheduled, analyst Alex Thorn said the crypto market structure bill could slip to September and run into midterm dynamics unless Senate leadership announces floor time by early July.

title: Vitalik Buterin calls obfuscation cryptography’s ‘final boss,’ but says current approaches remain wildly impractical
blurb: Vitalik Buterin published a blog post calling cryptographic obfuscation the "final boss of cryptography," focusing on indistinguishability obfuscation (iO) that lets a program run on cleartext inputs without revealing its code. Paired with a blockchain, iO could enable collusion-resistant voting and other protocols approaching a "trustless trusted third party." Buterin notes current constructions remain impractical, stacking primitives like FHE and lattice tools into runtimes he calls "literally galactic," with optimization, bolder assumptions, or non-lattice methods as paths forward.

title: New MiCA regime in Europe, U.S. jobs report for June: Crypto Week Ahead
blurb: The European Union's MiCA transitional period expires July 1, ending grandfathered operations for exchanges that haven't secured local licenses and prompting rivals to court Binance's regional users after the firm failed to obtain one. Traders also face a dense US macro week, with June nonfarm payrolls expected at 114K on July 2, a Robinhood product event, and Securitize's NYSE debut via SPAC.

title: How a Polymarket dispute over a single syllable ignited a bitter debate; Polymarket uses Risk Labs' Optimistic Oracle to decide ~200K tough call bets per month (David Segal/New York Times)
blurb: Polymarket settles roughly 200K disputed "tough call" markets each month through Risk Labs' Optimistic Oracle, the same UMA-built mechanism that recently turned a single-syllable wording dispute into a contested resolution. This NYT piece walks through how the optimistic oracle works in practice: proposers post outcomes, challengers stake bonds to dispute them, and UMA token holders vote to settle edge cases. It surfaces the structural tension in any human-arbitrated oracle, where ambiguous market language and token-weighted voting can decide which side gets paid.

title: Europe’s unlicensed crypto firms face ‘wipeout’ as MiCA deadline hits
blurb: ESMA called on unauthorized crypto-asset service providers to wind down operations in an orderly manner as MiCA's transitional period ends July 1, stripping pre-MiCA national registrations across the EEA. Of more than 3,000 registered VASPs in 2024, only 244 firms now hold MiCA licenses, and OKX Europe's CEO estimates 80% of players won't survive the combined licensing and compliance burden.

title: Galaxy Research cuts Clarity Act 2026 passage odds to 50%, citing Senate calendar concerns
blurb: Galaxy Research lowered its odds of the Clarity Act becoming law in 2026 to 50%, down from 60% earlier this month, citing a tightening Senate calendar and stalled negotiations rather than the crypto market-structure bill's substance. With no floor date set and President Trump's SAVE Act demand squeezing the schedule, Galaxy's Alex Thorn said passage could slip to September and run into midterm dynamics.

title: BIS warns stablecoins are more like ETFs than actual money, and they're creating FX risk
blurb: The Bank for International Settlements argued in its annual report that stablecoins behave more like ETF shares than money, citing secondary prices that drift from par and redemptions that aren't guaranteed to settle instantly at face value. The BIS also warned that dollar-pegged stablecoins are accelerating dollarization in vulnerable economies, undermining local currencies, evading capital controls, and creating FX risk without ever settling on central bank balance sheets.

title: AirTrunk Is Said to Be Near Confidential Filing for Biggest Singapore IPO in Years
blurb: AirTrunk, the Blackstone-backed data center operator, is near a confidential filing for a Singapore REIT IPO that would rank as the city-state's largest in years. The listing signals continued investor appetite for data center infrastructure as a yield-bearing asset class, channeling AI-driven compute demand into public-market real estate vehicles.

title: Europe’s unlicensed crypto firms face ‘wipeout’ as MiCA deadline hits
blurb: ESMA called on unauthorized crypto-asset service providers to wind down in an orderly manner as MiCA's transitional period ends July 1, when pre-MiCA national registrations expire across the EEA. Only 244 firms hold MiCA licenses against more than 3,000 registered VASPs in 2024, and OKX Europe's CEO estimates 80% of crypto players won't survive the combined MiCA, EMI, and payment-institution compliance burden.

title: India’s USDT premium tops 8.5% as crypto remittance crackdown squeezes stablecoin supply: report
blurb: India's USDT premium climbed above 8.5%, more than double its usual 3-4% range, after Enforcement Directorate raids on Bengaluru crypto remittance firms disrupted the stablecoin supply pipeline, per The Economic Times. The crackdown targets a roughly two-year-old workaround in which NRIs used USDT to bypass FEMA documentation requirements, and lands days before Parliament's finance committee meets the RBI on July 2 to weigh virtual-asset rules.

title: Czech cybersecurity startup Wultra lands €6.8 million to support Europe’s digital identity wallet rollout
blurb: Wultra, a Prague-based provider of post-quantum authentication and digital identity software for banks and fintechs, raised €6.8M in a Series A led by Seventure Partners. The startup will scale its quantum-resistant, passwordless authentication platform to support the European Digital Identity Wallet rollout, building on a base of 70-plus financial clients across 25 countries as institutions move to phishing-resistant, quantum-ready infrastructure.

title: Verizon, BT Merge International Units in Pivot to Home Markets
blurb: Verizon and BT Group agreed to form a joint venture combining their international business units, moving the low-margin operations off both carriers' balance sheets so each can concentrate on its home market. The structure lets Verizon double down on US wireless and BT on UK broadband and fiber, a retreat from global enterprise connectivity that has long underperformed their core consumer and domestic businesses.

title: Verizon and the UK's BT agree to create a joint venture for their international businesses with ~$4B in combined yearly revenue; Verizon will pay $625M to BT (Bloomberg)
blurb: Verizon and the UK's BT agreed to combine their international networking businesses into a joint venture with roughly $4B in combined annual revenue, with Verizon paying BT $625M to balance the contribution. The deal consolidates enterprise connectivity assets at a moment when both carriers are under pressure to cut costs and defend margins in a maturing global-telecom market.

title: Verizon Sees Quarterly Loss of Up to $800 Million Due to BT Deal
blurb: Verizon projected a second-quarter loss of $700M to $800M tied to a joint venture with BT Group that combines the two carriers' international businesses. The charge reflects the upfront cost of consolidating overseas operations into a shared entity, a structure that lets both telcos offload subscale international units while retaining exposure to any future upside.

title: New MiCA regime in Europe, U.S. jobs report for June: Crypto Week Ahead
blurb: The EU's MiCA transitional period expires July 1, ending the grandfathering window that let crypto firms operate without full authorization, with rivals now courting Binance users in the region after it failed to secure a license. The same week brings June nonfarm payrolls (est. 114K, down from 172K), Eurozone inflation, ISM manufacturing, and a Robinhood product reveal, setting up a macro-heavy stretch for digital-asset markets.
